summary: Ronacher argues that Python 3’s text model regressed from Python 2 by removing the byte-string type and tightening codecs in ways that break real-world workflows. He contends that Python 2’s Unicode handling was imperfect but pragmatic, offering flexible coercion and codecs that made interfacing with bytes workable. Python 3’s stricter separation of text and bytes, he says, led to loss of useful codecs, awkward APIs, and duplicated implementations in the standard library. He frames these changes as theoretical purity that fails against messy, byte-oriented reality. The post concludes that the migration narrative is overstated and that core developers should be more humble about the regressions many users experience.
key_points: Python 3 removed the byte-string `str` type, trading practical flexibility for a purist Unicode-only model.
Implicit coercion in Python 2 is risky but enables common workflows; Python 3’s removal of `.encode()`/`.decode()` on certain types created new pain without clear benefit.
The codec system in Python 2 was broadly useful, and Python 3’s rollback/reintroduction of codecs caused confusion and type errors.
APIs made “Unicode-only” in Python 3 were unrealistic for byte-heavy domains, forcing dual implementations and extra encode/decode churn.
Real-world migration data and developer sentiment suggest Python 3’s Unicode design undermined adoption and trust.
money_quotes: I will use this post to show that from the pure design of the language and standard library why Python 2 the better language for dealing with text and bytes.
From a purely theoretical point of view text always in Unicode sounds awesome. And it is. If your whole world is just your interpreter.
Let me be clear upfront: Python 2's way of dealing with Unicode is error prone and I am all in favour of improving it.
Confusing enough that Python 3 took the absolutely insanely radical step and removed `.decode()` from Unicode strings and `.encode()` from byte strings and caused me major frustration.
The idea that everything can be Unicode is nice in theory, but totally not applicable for the real world.
Python 3 is riddled with weird workarounds now for situations where you cannot use Unicode strings and for someone like me, who has to deal with those situations a lot, it's ridiculously annoying.
For 97% of us, Python 2 is our beloved world for years to come and telling us constantly about how amazing Python 3 is not just painful, it's also wrong in light of the many regressions.
key_insight: Python 3’s Unicode purity introduced real-world regressions in byte handling and tooling ergonomics, making the transition feel less like progress and more like a practical step backward.
